design_philosophy.core_loop: 2-on-2 arcade basketball with exaggerated physics: fast breaks, dunking, physical contact without penalty, momentum-based scoring
design_philosophy.primary_tension: momentum swings between teams; 'on fire' status creates escalating scoring potential; physical contact-based disruption of opponent plays
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: superhuman athleticism: jumping far above human capability, executing impossible dunks, dominating through physical play without fouls
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Deliberately rejects simulation basketball in favor of arcade spectacle; celebrates exaggeration, showboating, and unrealistic physicality; eliminates traditional fouling consequences; positions basketball as entertainment over sport

difficulty.checkpointing: Quarter-based structure allows resumption at quarter boundaries
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Team selection and player attribute variation provide difficulty modulation; arcade cabinet context suggests adjustable difficulty settings typical of era

progression.upgrade_system: Player roster selection with varying attributes; secret characters unlock through play
progression.unlock_mechanism: Secret characters discoverable during play sessions; player statistics tracked between sessions enabling progression narratives
replayability_hooks: Player statistics and record tracking across sessions

world_building_techniques: NBA licensing provides real team/player identity
Announcer commentary (Tim Kitzrow) creates broadcast authenticity layer
Arcade presentation style (digitized graphics, over-the-top visual effects)

faction_indicators: NBA team affiliation (real licensed teams create player investment)
Player roster choice signals basketball preference
environmental_storytelling: Arcade cabinet setting in public spaces signals competitive social play
High-score persistence creates local legend-building potential

sources_used: wikipedia: NBA Jam series overview, 1993 arcade original and Genesis port documentation
